title: Unit Final: Budgeting, Saving, and Money Management

questions.prompt: Which statement best explains the purpose of a budget?

choices.text: A budget helps organize income, spending, and saving decisions
choices.feedback: Correct. A budget supports financial decisions by organizing the full money plan.

choices.text: A budget guarantees that every purchase is wise
choices.feedback: A budget helps evaluate purchases, but it does not guarantee every decision is wise.

choices.text: A budget only matters after all money has already been spent
choices.feedback: A budget is most useful before and during financial decisions.

choices.text: A budget replaces the need for saving goals
choices.feedback: Saving goals are part of a strong budget rather than something the budget replaces.

questions.prompt: A student has $140 in weekly income. Planned categories are $35 transportation, $30 meals, $20 savings, and $18 school expenses. How much remains?

choices.feedback: Check the sum of the planned categories and subtract carefully.
Correct. The planned total is $103, and $140 minus $103 leaves $37.
This is too high after all planned categories are included.
That total does not match the income and expense amounts in the scenario.

questions.prompt: An emergency fund is part of financial planning because it helps prepare for unexpected expenses.

choices.text: True
choices.feedback: Correct. Emergency funds help protect financial stability when unplanned costs happen.

choices.text: False
choices.feedback: Emergency funds are specifically meant to help with unexpected expenses.

questions.prompt: A student wants easy access to spending money for the week and also wants to keep money set aside for later. Which account choice is strongest?

choices.text: Use only savings for both purposes because it is all money
choices.feedback: The strongest decision matches the account type to the financial purpose.

choices.text: Use savings for weekly spending and checking for money set aside for later
choices.feedback: That setup reverses the stronger purpose of each account type.

choices.text: Keep all money in one place so there is no need to organize by purpose
choices.feedback: Separating money by purpose can support stronger financial decisions.

choices.text: Use checking for weekly spending and savings for money set aside for later
choices.feedback: Correct. Checking supports access, while savings supports money held for future use.

questions.prompt: A student wants to save $360 for emergencies and can save $30 each month. How long will it take to reach the goal?

choices.text: 10 months
choices.feedback: Divide the total goal by the monthly saving amount carefully.

choices.text: 11 months
choices.feedback: This is too short. Divide the savings goal by the monthly amount carefully.

choices.text: 12 months
choices.feedback: Correct. $360 divided by $30 equals 12 months.

choices.text: 14 months
choices.feedback: That timeline is longer than needed for this savings goal.

questions.prompt: A student has $48 left in the weekly budget and wants to make an unplanned $34 purchase. Which conclusion is strongest?

choices.text: The purchase is automatically strong because there is enough money at the moment
choices.feedback: A purchase can fit mathematically and still weaken the budget.

choices.text: The purchase leaves only $14, so the student should evaluate whether that reduced flexibility is worth it
choices.feedback: Correct. The strongest conclusion compares the purchase to the financial consequences.

choices.text: The purchase improves the emergency fund
choices.feedback: An unplanned purchase usually reduces available money instead of improving savings.

choices.text: The purchase means the full budget no longer matters
choices.feedback: The full budget still matters when judging the strength of a decision.

questions.prompt: A strong money decision should compare short-term wants with long-term priorities.

choices.text: False
choices.feedback: Strong financial decisions do compare immediate wants with long-term goals and stability.

choices.text: True
choices.feedback: Correct. Good financial reasoning considers both immediate and future consequences.

questions.prompt: Scenario: Jada earns $125 this week. She plans $28 for transportation, $24 for meals, $18 for savings, and $16 for school expenses. She is considering a $25 impulse purchase. What is the strongest recommendation?

choices.text: The impulse purchase should happen automatically because some money remains
choices.feedback: A strong recommendation should compare the purchase to the remaining budget and priorities.

choices.text: The planned total is $86, so $39 remains before the purchase; that means the purchase should happen automatically
choices.feedback: That skips the key question of whether only $14 remaining after the purchase is a strong choice.

choices.text: The planned total is $86, so $39 remains before the purchase and $14 would remain after it; Jada should decide whether that reduced cushion is worth it
choices.feedback: Correct. The strongest recommendation uses the numbers and explains the consequence of the added spending.

choices.text: The purchase should be judged without using the budget numbers
choices.feedback: Budget numbers are necessary for a justified financial recommendation.

questions.prompt: Scenario: Mateo wants to keep $100 available for weekly spending and save $35 each month for emergencies. Which financial setup is strongest?

choices.text: Use checking for both purposes so no organization is needed
choices.feedback: The strongest setup usually matches account type to financial purpose.

choices.text: Use checking for both purposes because organization is not important
choices.feedback: Organization by purpose does matter, especially when one goal is weekly spending and the other is emergency savings.

choices.text: Use savings for weekly spending and checking for emergencies
choices.feedback: That setup reverses the stronger purpose of each account type.

choices.text: Use checking for weekly spending and savings for the emergency goal because the purposes are different
choices.feedback: Correct. This setup matches each financial purpose to the stronger account type.

questions.prompt: Scenario: A student has a goal to save $240 in 8 months. Which monthly saving amount best matches the plan?

choices.text: $20 per month
choices.feedback: Multiply the monthly amount by 8 months and compare it to the total goal.

choices.text: $25 per month
choices.feedback: This total would not fully reach the $240 goal in 8 months.

choices.text: $30 per month
choices.feedback: Correct. $30 times 8 months equals $240.

choices.text: $35 per month
choices.feedback: That amount would exceed the target rather than match it exactly.
